children.text: Diana Montejano is the daughter of Juan and María Luisa Montejano. She has one older brother, David, and two younger brothers, Johnny and Jerry. Her family was from Del Rio, Texas, a small town about 150 miles west of San Antonio. María Luisa only had a fourth grade education, as her father had pulled her and her siblings out of school due to intense harassment from Anglo students and had them begin work as migrant laborers instead. Juan graduated high school, but he did so in a segregated system.

At the time, the 1930 Del Rio ISD v. Salvatierra ruling meant that as long as the school claimed it was separating Mexican American students due to “pedagogical” reasons (despite the fact that it was mostly racial), the segregation was fully legal. “Mexican schools,” the schools that the Mexican American children were sent to, provided insubstantial education compared to their Anglo counterparts. In addition, they forbid the teaching of Spanish, and students could be harshly punished if they were caught speaking it on school grounds, even if they were outside of class. These punishments could range from anything to detention and suspension to ear-pinchings and paddlings. It created generational trauma which affected Montejano’s family for generations, and her entire family from her grandfather to her had to consciously resist in order to keep their Spanish.

Diana Montejano was born in Del Rio in 1950, but she grew up in San Antonio’s historically Mexican American Westside. The area that became San Antonio has been home to humans for thousands of years, most notably the indigenous Coahuiltecan peoples. The Spanish colonized it in 1718, but it didn’t really start to grow until it was part of the United States with the arrival of the International and Great Northern Railroad in 1883. That railroad brought industrialization to the city, and once even greater populations migrated there in the 1930s, segregation, too.

These maps are from the Home Owners Loan Corporation (HOLC), which was created in 1933. They produced maps of every residential area in the United States, grading neighborhoods by color of what was most “risky” for banks to give mortgages to and for realtors to sell homes to. This process was known as “redlining.” A neighborhood could be graded as “Best,” “Still Desirable,” “Definitely Declining,” or “Hazardous.” The riskiness was determined by race. If a neighborhood had Black and Brown residents, then it would be graded as “Hazardous” by the HOLC, regardless of the class level of the people living there. The first map of San Antonio shows the “Hazardous” areas mostly concentrated to the West/Southwest and East sides of the city.

content.caption: “HOLC San Antonio City Survey Report 1 Exhibit A: Grades of Security.” A hand colored "residential security map" created to indicate levels of security for real estate investment in San Antonio neighborhoods. Map courtesy of the University of Texas at San Antonio Libraries Special Collections.

children.text: The second map, also created by the HOLC, shows the racial concentrations of San Antonio, with three categories for “Negro,” “Mexican,” and “White.” As you can see, the “Hazardous” neighborhoods above match up almost perfectly with the “Mexican” and “Negro” concentrations below.

content.caption: “HOLC San Antonio City Survey Report 1 Exhibit B: Racial Concentrations.” Hand colored map showing racial concentrations in San Antonio neighborhoods. Color codes are split into 3 groups: "Negro," "Mexican," and "White." Map courtesy of the University of Texas at San Antonio Libraries Special Collections.

children.text: Redlining had a profound effect on where Diana Montejano and her family could live. When her father purchased a home on the Westside in the late 1950s, he was forced to select one south of Culebra St., as it was the dividing line between the Mexican American and Anglo Neighborhoods. Mexicans could not purchase homes anywhere that was not deemed “Hazardous.”
Prayer of a Raging Storm
Diana L. Montejano
(From “Paginas de Mi Vida”)
I prayed, half-believing in judgment day,
a prayer uttered in between thunder-booms,
in between the lightening X-raying the rooms
in my house, and I on my knees, doubted that
my unpremeditated inflections could ever be
heard above the anger of a rising river:
En el nombre del Padre --
My father is Juan, changed to John
somewhere along the way, my mother,
Maria Luisa, is now called Mary Lou --
y el Hijo --
I have three strong brothers --
David, spelled the same in Spanish
or English --
Juan Jr. now became Johnny Jr.,
I just call him Junior --
Jerry, who some call "Geraldo,"
"Gerardo," or "Gerónimo," but my parents
say Jerry is his only name –
y yo soy Dianna, hija de Juan y María Luisa,
hermana de David, Junior, y Jerry --
In school, my name became an English Diane,
but I always knew the difference --
y el Espirítu Santo --
Estos son mis abuelos: Alvino y Francisca,
my father's parents who died when he was nine,
y mi memoria of them is limited to their faces
in brown ovals -- and to their names.
Telésforo y María del Refugio son mis abuelos
on my mother's side. I never met my grandmother
with these piercing, frozen eyes, never smiling.
She died when my mother was fourteen. There are
rumores, the kind that run entre familias,
que mi abuela's second husband (not my abuelo),
had done the abortion himself, back-alley style,
and she had bled slowly to death -- la verdad
remains lost in her eyes.
Papá Telésforo, the only grandparent I knew, lived a humble-left-over-rebel-from-the-Pancho-Villa-days'
life, picking the Earth and his hand-made violin,
siempre cantando la "Prieta Linda," and it became
my song when my adopted grandmother talked of
how bonito el güero o la güera was, y nunca la
comprendí -- I could never believe it was love that
made her tell me over and over to marry a blue-eyed
güero, so I could desafinar la sangre -- thin out
the blood. No, this never became my prayer.

children.caption: Diana Montejano and her three brothers: Jerry, Johnny Junior, and David Montejano.

title: Part I: Family and Early Life on the Westside
